 Ancient Spanish religious activities 
 March 19 to October 12, Spanish Bullfighting Festival 
 Terminology and Alternate Names 
 Spanish bullfighting is commonly referred to as "toreo" (pronunciation: [to'ɾeo]) in Spanish, also known as "corrida de toros" or "tauromaquia". Its synonymous expressions include "la fiesta brava" and "tauromaquia". 
 Associated terms include "torero" (bullfighter), "taurino" (taurine, related to bullfighting), "toro" (bull), "matador" (matador), "muleta" (the red cape or stick), "ruedo" (bullring), "lidia" (the bullfight), "corrida" (the running of the bulls or the bullfight), and "faena" (the work or the final stage of the bullfight performance). 
 Bullfighting, known in Spanish as toreo, corrida de toros, or tauromaquia, and in Portuguese as tourada, corrida de touros, or tauromaquia, is a sport involving a confrontation between humans and a bull. The participants are called bullfighters (toreros). It is primarily popular in Spain, Portugal, and Latin America, and is considered the national spectacle of Spain. The history of bullfighting can be traced back to prehistoric bull worship and cave paintings. The direct origin of modern Spanish bullfighting can be traced to the Middle Ages, evolving from a combination of aristocratic hunting and knightly tournaments. As a documented ritual activity, it began in the 13th century during the reign of King Alfonso X of Spain as a religious sacrificial ceremony, later gradually evolving into a bullfighting spectacle.

[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: This article needs additional citations for verification. (May 2015) | 
 Spanish-style bullfighting is a type of bullfighting that is practiced in several Spanish-speaking countries: Spain, Mexico, Ecuador, Venezuela, Peru, as well as in parts of southern France and Portugal. In Colombia it has been outlawed but is being phased out with a full ban coming in effect in 2027. This style of bullfighting involves a physical contest with humans (and other animals) attempting to publicly subdue, immobilize, or kill a bull. The most common bull used is the Spanish Fighting Bull (Toro Bravo), a type of cattle native to the Iberian Peninsula. This style of bullfighting is seen to be both a sport and performance art. The red colour of the cape is a matter of tradition—bulls are color blind. They attack moving objects; the brightly colored cape is used to mask blood stains.

[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: [edit]Spanish-style bullfighting is called corrida de toros (literally "coursing of bulls") or la fiesta ("the festival"). In the traditional corrida, three matadores each fight two bulls, each of which is between four and six years old and weighs no less than 460 kg (1,014 lb).[23] Each matador has six assistants: two picadores (lancers mounted on horseback), three banderilleros – who along with the matadors are collectively known as toreros (bullfighters) – and a mozo de espadas (sword page). Collectively they comprise a cuadrilla (entourage). In Spanish the more general torero or diestro (literally 'right-hander') is used for the lead fighter, and only when needed to distinguish a man is the full title matador de toros used; in English, "matador" is generally used for the bullfighter.
